banner: Week 26 · Postulates, Theorems & the Angle Relationships Behind Them
essential_question: Every angle rule you've used all year has always been true — but which ones are simply assumed, and which ones can actually be PROVEN from the others?
objective: Distinguish a postulate (assumed) from a theorem (proven) and a corollary, and name the specific postulate or theorem that justifies vertical angles, linear pairs, and corresponding/alternate angles on parallel lines.

content.prompt: Vertical angles are congruent — you've used that fact since Lesson 6. But is it a RULE we just agreed to, or something we could actually prove using an even simpler rule? Before today's lesson, guess: is 'vertical angles are congruent' a postulate or a theorem — and why?

content.heading: Sorted by how we know they're true
bullets: A postulate (or axiom) is a statement we accept as true without proof — a starting rule of the system.
A theorem is a statement PROVEN true using postulates, definitions, and earlier theorems.
A corollary is a statement that follows almost immediately from a theorem, needing little extra proof.
The Linear Pair Postulate: a linear pair is always supplementary. We accept this — it's what a straight angle IS.
The Vertical Angles Theorem: vertical angles are congruent. This is PROVEN from the Linear Pair Postulate, not assumed — two angles supplementary to the SAME angle must be equal.
The Corresponding Angles Postulate (parallel lines cut by a transversal) is accepted; the Alternate Interior and Alternate Exterior Angles Theorems are then PROVEN from it.

content.heading: Drag the figure; watch the justifying rule light up
content.note: On the sim, switch between 'Two Crossing Lines' and 'Parallel Lines + Transversal.' Drag the gold handle and read which NAMED postulate or theorem justifies each equal or supplementary pair. Which rules are postulates (assumed)? Which are theorems (proven) — and proven from what?

content.preview: Build session: build a theorem-card reference wall and test it with a shuffle-and-match round.

success_criteria.text: I can define postulate, theorem, and corollary, and give an example of each from this course
I can name the specific postulate or theorem that justifies a given angle relationship — linear pair, vertical angles, corresponding angles, alternate interior/exterior angles
I can write the converse of a postulate or theorem and state whether it is also guaranteed true

questions.prompt: A statement accepted as true without proof is called a ___.
questions.explain: A postulate (or axiom) is one of the starting rules we simply agree to accept — no proof required.

questions.prompt: Which of these is PROVEN using a postulate, rather than simply assumed?
options: the definition of a right angle
questions.explain: A theorem must be proven from postulates, definitions, and earlier theorems — the Vertical Angles Theorem is proven from the Linear Pair Postulate.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain the difference between a postulate and a theorem, and give one example of each from this course.
questions.answer: A postulate is accepted as true without proof (e.g., the Linear Pair Postulate: a linear pair is always supplementary). A theorem must be proven using postulates and other theorems (e.g., the Vertical Angles Theorem, proven from the Linear Pair Postulate).
questions.explain: Postulates are where the logical chain starts; theorems are what gets built from that starting point.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw two lines crossing at a point H, forming ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 in order around H. Mark one linear pair and label it with the postulate that makes it supplementary; mark the ∠1 & ∠3 vertical pair and label it with the theorem that makes it congruent.
questions.answer: Two crossing lines at H with four labeled angles; one adjacent pair marked 'Linear Pair Postulate — supplementary' and the ∠1/∠3 pair marked 'Vertical Angles Theorem — congruent'.
questions.explain: Rubric: correctly drawn and labeled crossing figure (1 pt) + both pairs correctly matched to their rule's actual name (1 pt).

questions.prompt: 'If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, corresponding angles are congruent' — in this course, is that statement a postulate or a theorem?
questions.explain: The Corresponding Angles Postulate is accepted outright as a defining property of parallel lines; the alternate interior and alternate exterior congruences are then PROVEN from it — they're theorems.

questions.prompt: Alternate interior angles are congruent when two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. Is this a postulate or a theorem in this course — and what two rules would you chain together to prove it?
questions.answer: It's a theorem. Chain the Vertical Angles Theorem (∠4 ≅ ∠1, same crossing) with the Corresponding Angles Postulate (∠1 ≅ ∠5) to get ∠4 ≅ ∠5 — an alternate interior pair, proven, not assumed.
questions.explain: This is exactly the chained argument from Lesson 7's concept note, now with both links named.

questions.prompt: Exit: name one geometry fact you've used all year (vertical angles congruent, a linear pair sums to 180°, corresponding angles congruent, etc.) and say whether you now think it's a postulate or a theorem — and why.
